sections.heading: Project Overview
sections.content:
The aim of this project was to develop an access control system consisting of a keypad, servo motor and three LEDs to depict the unlock state of the system, all coded in AVR Assembly.

The system needed to compare a user-inputted code against a stored code to determine the unlock state of the system.

A basic lock system was successfully created, although it did not meet all the requirements of the project, as it only had a lock and unlock state, and did not give an opportunity to re-enter the code.

sections.heading: My Contribution
sections.content: This was a solo project, so all contributions were made by me.

sections.heading: Implementation & Technical Details
sections.content:
This project involved the development of a keypad-based access control system programmed entirely in AVR Assembly for an ATmega328P microcontroller.

The system utilised a 4×3 matrix keypad to collect a four-digit passcode, store user input in memory buffers, and compare it against a predefined code using pointer-based memory traversal and byte-by-byte verification.

Visual feedback was provided through a series of LEDs that displayed input progress and indicated successful or failed authentication states.

Keypad scanning, debouncing, and key identification were implemented using interrupts and lookup tables, while user input was managed through a custom buffering system.

Although the original specification included a servo-driven lock mechanism and a secondary authentication attempt state, the final implementation focused on achieving reliable passcode entry and verification, as the original specification could not be achieved before deadline.
